identity.research_question: **Description: \[Published\]** Custom computational package featuring a knowledge-based, multi-stage single-cell classification algorithm that uses logical gating trees of marker expressions to annotate cell types in multiplexed imaging datasets (e.g. t-CycIF) without manual labeling or hard thresho
identity.project_summary: **Description: \[Published\]** Custom computational package featuring a knowledge-based, multi-stage single-cell classification algorithm that uses logical gating trees of marker expressions to annotate cell types in multiplexed imaging datasets (e.g. t-CycIF) without manual labeling or hard thresholds. Published in *Bioinformatics* (doi:10.1093/bioinformatics/btaf082). Benchmarked against three state-of-the-art classifiers on four public datasets with ground truth, demonstrating superior accuracy and a runtime reduction of an order of magnitude. Performance validated on two large whole-slide imaging datasets from the lab. Code is open-sourced at [farkkilab/tribus](https://github.com/farkkilab/tribus), with benchmarking data and notebooks hosted on [Synapse (syn63884734)](https://www.synapse.org/Synapse:syn63884734).
identity.responsible: Ziqi Kang (Angela Szabo, Teodora Farago, Fernando Perez, Ada Junquera, Saundarya Shah, Inga-Maria Launonen, Ella Anttila, Julia Casado, Kevin Elias, Anni Virtanen, Ulla-Maija Haltia, Anniina Färkkilä)

**Ziqi Kang^1∗^, Angela Szabo^1^, Teodora Farago^1^, Inga-Maria
Launonen^1^, Fernando Perez^1^, Ella Anttila^1^, Kevin Elias^2^, Julia
Casado^1^, Peter Sorger^3^, Anniina Färkkilä^1,\ 4,\ 5,\ 6^**

^1^ Research Program in Systems Oncology, University of Helsinki,
Finland

^2^ Division of Gynecologic Oncology, Brigham and Women's Hospital,
Harvard Medical School, MA, USA

^3^ Laboratory of Systems Pharmacology, Harvard Medical School, MA, USA

^4^ Institute for Molecular Medicine Finland, Helsinki Institute of Life
Sciences, University of Helsinki, Finland

^5^ Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, Helsinki University
Hospital, Finland

^6^ iCAN Digital Precision Cancer Medicine Flagship, Finland.

Correspondence should be addressed to A.F.
([[anniina.farkkila@helsinki.fi]{.underline}](mailto:anniina.farkkila@helsinki.fi)).

## Introduction

Multiplexed imaging at single-cell resolution is becoming widely used to
decipher the role of the cellular microenvironment in cancer and other
complex diseases. To identify spatial patterns of single cells on a
tissue, accurate cell-type phenotyping is a crucial step. We present
Tribus, an interactive, knowledge-based classifier that avoids hard-set
thresholds and manual labeling, is robust to noise, and takes fewer
iterations from the user than current methods of labeling.

## Materials and Methods

fier that avoids hard-set
thresholds and manual labeling, is robust to noise, and takes fewer
iterations from the user than current methods of labeling.

## Materials and Methods

We developed Tribus, an open-source Python 3 package utilizing
self-organizing maps for data clustering with an integrated Napari
plug-in for fast visualization. It assigns the cell phenotypes based on
user-defined label description tables and median marker expressions.
Tribus has built-in visualization functions to gain insight into the
input data and to evaluate the results. The Napari plug-in provides a
user-friendly way to visualize the results and perform quality control.
To explore the performance of Tribus, we used 1) manually expert
annotated datasets from different tissue origins 2) datasets with
cluster-based annotation 3) previously unpublished datasets. We
benchmarked Tribus with two other similar prior knowledge-based tools:
ACDC and Astir.

## Results

Tribus recovers fine-grained cell types as accurately as human experts
on a MIBI-TOF Ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) dataset with a 37-plex
antibody staining panel and 23 cell types. Tribus can also target
ambiguous populations and discover phenotypically new subtypes in the
DCIS dataset, such as CD36+CD31+ fibroblast and HER2-tumor luminal
subtypes. Tribus outperforms other methods comparing efficiency and
accuracy.

phenotypically new subtypes in the
DCIS dataset, such as CD36+CD31+ fibroblast and HER2-tumor luminal
subtypes. Tribus outperforms other methods comparing efficiency and
accuracy. Benchmarking on four public mass cytometry and multiplexed
imaging datasets all with ground truth labels, Tribus reaches high
accuracy compared with ACDC and Astir but with a shorter running time in
one order of magnitude. Tribus yields rapid and accurate cell type
calling in a large whole slide imaging dataset, consisting of over a
million cells from five matched HGSC samples collected before and after
neoadjuvant chemotherapy (NACT).

## Conclusion

Overall, Tribus is a novel cell-type classification tool to analyze
different sizes of datasets across different technologies, resulting in
accelerated analysis of the spatial tumor microenvironment (TME). Tribus
shows advantages in running times, accuracy, user-friendly and
reproducibility.

**Multiplexed imaging at single-cell resolution has been widely used to

complex diseases. Accurate cell-type phenotyping based on marker
combinations is crucial, but challenging because of (i) fluorescence
noise and batch effects, (ii) segmentation artifacts, (iii) laborious
annotation of the ground truth, and (iv) difficulty in reproducing
human-biased thresholding. Here, we present Tribus, an interactive
knowledge-based classifier designed for multiplexed image and proteomic
datasets that avoids hard-set thresholds and manual labeling. The
performance of Tribus was validated by benchmarking it with two similar
methods on four public datasets with manual labels. Tribus can recover

the discovery of phenotypically new subtypes. Two large in-house

# INTRODUCTION

Multiplexed imaging techniques at single-cell resolution levels, such as
cyclic immunofluorescence (CyCIF) [(Lin et al.,
2018)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?KROv5k), co-detection by

ed imaging techniques at single-cell resolution levels, such as
cyclic immunofluorescence (CyCIF) [(Lin et al.,
2018)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?KROv5k), co-detection by
indexing (CODEX) [(Black et al.,
2021)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?JGGE22), and multiplexed ion
beam imaging by time of flight (MIBI-TOF) [(Keren et al.,
2019)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?QIwFvh), have developed
significant advantages for understanding tissue architecture. Such
techniques allow researchers to measure dozens of proteins at
single-cell resolution while retaining the spatial information of origin
in the tissue section. These experimental workflows generate datasets
that include multiple features and provide novel insights into the
diversity of cellular phenotypes and tissue behaviors [(Spitzer & Nolan,
2016)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?8HFLes).

Cell-type annotation has become one of the most essential tasks and a
bottleneck for gaining full potential from multiplexed imaging
technologies. The main obstacles to cell-type phenotyping on multiplexed
images are the unexpected combinations of markers, signal noise, and
reproducibility limit caused by imperfect cell segmentation,
pre-processing artifacts, or tissue processing artifacts, such as
autofluorescence. It is difficult to apply popular scRNA-seq cell-type

mit caused by imperfect cell segmentation,
pre-processing artifacts, or tissue processing artifacts, such as
autofluorescence. It is difficult to apply popular scRNA-seq cell-type
callers [(Xie et al., 2021)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?QShDIL)
to multiplexed imaging datasets because they are not designed to
overcome segmentation noise, which does not exist in scRNA-seq data. The
order-of-magnitude difference in the number of features is also an
indispensable problem. Several markers can dominate the clustering
process in multiplexed imaging techniques, and thousands of genes have
been provided to scRNA-seq callers to overcome this problem.

Gating [(Staats et al.,
2019)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?7mgQgW)is a classical
cell-type annotation method that requires manual identification of cell
types based on the pattern of features visualized by histograms and
bivariate plots. However, with the increasing size of the marker panel,
manual gating becomes time-consuming and sensitive to batch effects
[(Verschoor et al., 2015)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?beDAnS),
which is no longer feasible. Unsupervised clustering tools such as
PhenoGraph [(Levine et al.,
2015)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?fqmsl5), Leiden [(Traag et
al., 2019)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?U71hm0), and DBscan

tools such as
PhenoGraph [(Levine et al.,
2015)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?fqmsl5), Leiden [(Traag et
al., 2019)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?U71hm0), and DBscan
[(Ester et al., 1996)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?L0oHCj) have
been applied to perform automatic data exploration [(Liu et al.,
2019)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?hnJI58). However, manual
checking is still required to assign meaningful cell types to the
obtained clusters. To perform a deeper profiling of cell types, it is
usually necessary to perform over-clustering and then merge the
clusters, which is both computationally and time-consuming. An
alternative approach is to use supervised or semi-supervised machine
learning methods [(Abdelaal et al.,
2019)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?49sLMo), which were developed
to perform accurate auto cell-type classification. However, such methods
require pre-training on manually gated datasets according to various
experimental designs, thus requiring extra human work and lacking
extendability.

Here, we introduce the design of a novel cell-type caller named the
Tribus. Tribus performs reproducible automatic cell-type annotations
across variable high-plex imaging and proteomic datasets without manual
thresholding. Tribus incorporates a widely used self-organizing map
(SOM) [(Kohonen, 1982)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?Yl4HID)

aging and proteomic datasets without manual
thresholding. Tribus incorporates a widely used self-organizing map
(SOM) [(Kohonen, 1982)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?Yl4HID)
unsupervised neural network model with a unique scoring function for
assigning cell types according to prior biological knowledge. An
integrated Napari plugin allows for fast user interaction and curation.
We validated the accuracy of Tribus on four public multiplexed imaging
and non-spatial proteomic datasets generated by CODEX and the mass
cytometry methods. We compared the performance of Tribus with two
similar knowledge-based cell-type identification approaches: ACDC [(Lee
et al., 2017)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?J4be42) and Astir
[(Geuenich et al., 2021)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?zlhfxw).
We demonstrate the utility of Tribus in the analysis of two in-house
CyCIF datasets. Overall, Tribus represents a new framework for
semi-automated cell-type calling for multiplexed imaging and proteomics
techniques.

# Methods

## The structure of Tribus

To run Tribus, a marker expression table and a prior knowledge-based
logic table are required as inputs. The logic table can be defined as a
data matrix $L(c_{i},\ m_{j})$ containing values
$\lbrack - 1,\ 0,\ 1\rbrack$, where $c_{i}$ is the cell type
${i_{th}}^{}$ and $m_{j}$ is the marker ${j_{th}}^{}$. If a marker is

data matrix $L(c_{i},\ m_{j})$ containing values
$\lbrack - 1,\ 0,\ 1\rbrack$, where $c_{i}$ is the cell type
${i_{th}}^{}$ and $m_{j}$ is the marker ${j_{th}}^{}$. If a marker is
present in a specific cell type, the value in the logic table is
assigned a value of 1. If a marker is supposed to be absent in a
specific cell type, then the value in the logic table should be assigned
as -1. For neutral or unknown markers, a score of 0 was assigned. Each
cell type should at least have one positive marker in the logic table
(Supplementary Table 1-3).

Tribus comprises two main parts. First, Tribus uses unsupervised
learning to find clusters from the input data, and then assigns each
cluster to a certain cell type based on the logic table. The cell type
assignment is performed hierarchically, which means Tribus first assigns
lower-level cell types and then assigns higher-level cell types to
create more precise categories.

## Unsupervised learning in Tribus

Tribus uses a self-organizing map (SOM) for clustering based on the
MiniSOM package [(Vettigli,
2013/2023)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?ghMcaA). SOM can
represent an input space with high dimensions as a map space, which
consists of components called \"nodes\". The quantization error (Q) was
used to evaluate the algorithm performance, which was calculated by

h high dimensions as a map space, which
consists of components called \"nodes\". The quantization error (Q) was
used to evaluate the algorithm performance, which was calculated by
determining the average distance of the sample vectors to the cluster
centroids.

A comparison of quantization errors needs to be performed under the same
grid size [(Pölzlbauer,
n.d.)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?7mS4PU). To determine the
optimal grid size, we used the approach of Vesanto [(Vesanto &
Alhoniemi, 2000)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?KEe5Lj): $G = 5$,
where $N$ is the input data size (i.e., number of cells). The parameters
of the SOM, including $\sigma$ (the spread of the neighborhood function)
and learning rate, were optimized by minimizing the quantization error.
Hyperparameter tuning was implemented based on the package hyperopt
[(Bergstra et al., 2013)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?fTIX2g),
where the objective function was to minimize quantization error, and the
search space for $\sigma$ and learning rate ranged from 0.001 to 5.

## Cell type assignment by scoring functions

After SOM clustering, a node matrix $N(n_{k},\ v_{j})$ is generated,
where $v_{j}$ is calculated using the median expression of marker
$m_{j}$ in $n_{k}$. We designed a scoring function based on the concept
of squared error implemented in the QueryStarPlot function of the
FlowSOM package [(Van Gassen et al.,
2015)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?G09YkV). The function
calculates the score $s_{i}$ of a certain cell type $i$ for each node
$k$.

## Napari plug-in

To efficiently assess and explore cell-type labeling results, we
developed a custom plugin integrated with the Napari [(Ahlers, Jannis et
al., 2023)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?ZIFqeC) framework. This
allows the user to run Tribus over one sample at a time, display the
results simultaneously, or load previously saved data. We incorporated
the ZARR format to overcome the computational limitations associated
with large datasets.

The key functionalities of the Napari plugin include the following.

\(i\) Cell-type mask visualization: The plugin sorts and displays
different cell-type labels as separate layers using visually distinct
colors, allowing the user to overlay them with the imaging data for
quality control. This function is also available in a stand-alone
Jupyter Notebook.

\(ii\) Probability score visualization: Cell masks are represented as a
color gradient of the probability score assigned by the algorithm. This
allows the user to identify and check ambiguous cells. This allows the
user to identify and check ambiguous cells and assess the assigned

ity score assigned by the algorithm. This
allows the user to identify and check ambiguous cells. This allows the
user to identify and check ambiguous cells and assess the assigned
"other" and "undefined" thresholds.

\(iii\) Marker intensity visualization: The median expression levels of
the desired markers are represented through gradient shading on the
segmentation mask. This allows the user to visually assess the results
and look for potential biases.

## Benchmarking Datasets

We chose four public mass cytometry and multiplexed imaging datasets
with ground truth labels to test the performance of Tribus.

### AML

This dataset contains single-cell proteomic profiles of human bone
marrow from patients with acute myeloid leukemia (AML) and healthy adult
donors [(Levine et al.,
2015)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?a3cXqw). The AML dataset was
collected via mass cytometry using a panel of 32 markers targeting 14
manually gated cell classes. When performing benchmarking, the
\"NotDebrisSinglets\" cell type was removed from the analysis (N =
104184 cells). The cell typing logic table was designed based on the
cell type-marker table provided in ACDC research.

### BMMC

The BMMC dataset was obtained from bone marrow mononuclear cells
[(Bendall et al., 2011)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?NaqDnh) and
profiled using mass cytometry. The publicly available dataset contains

from bone marrow mononuclear cells
[(Bendall et al., 2011)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?NaqDnh) and
profiled using mass cytometry. The publicly available dataset contains
13 markers and 24 manually gated cell populations. Following ACDC
research, erythroblasts, megakaryocyte platelets, and myelocytes were
merged as an unknown population and removed from the analysis. All
\"NotGated\" cells were also removed (N = 61725 cells). The cell typing
logic table was modified based on the cell type-marker table provided in
ACDC research.

### DCIS

The ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) dataset was collected using
multiplexed ion beam imaging by time-of-flight (MIBI-TOF) with a 37-plex
antibody staining panel. DCIS consists of 69672 cells, 23 cell types
from tissues containing 79 clinically annotated surgical resections
[(Risom et al., 2022)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?dJSFis),
including normal breast tissue (N = 9, reduction mammoplasty), primary
DCIS (N = 58), and IBC (N = 12). The cell-typing logic table for DCIS
was also generated based on the panel design in the published research.

### HuBMAP

HuBMAP is a published co-detection by indexing (CODEX) imaging dataset
collected from nine donors with a 54-plex panel targeting 25 cell types
[(Hickey et al., 2023)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?SrSjJ5). The

y indexing (CODEX) imaging dataset
collected from nine donors with a 54-plex panel targeting 25 cell types
[(Hickey et al., 2023)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?SrSjJ5). The
HuBMAP dataset includes eight regions (in the order of trajectory from
the stomach): duodenum, proximal jejunum, mid-jejunum, and ileum from
the small bowel, and ascending, transverse, descending, and sigmoid

information provided in published studies.

### In-house Datasets

To further validate the performance of Tribus, we also applied Tribus to
two in-house high-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC) datasets generated
by tissue-based cyclic immunofluorescence (tCyCIF). The HRP dataset
contained 21 homologous recombination-proficient (HRP) HGSC samples
stained with a 14-plex antibody panel (Supplementary Table 4). The NACT
dataset contains three images, including two tumor sections after
neoadjuvant chemotherapy (NACT) and one treatment-naive biopsy. The NACT
dataset was stained using a 36-plex antibody panel (Supplementary Table
5).

Cell segmentation was carried out using StarDist, and the mean

nd one treatment-naive biopsy. The NACT
dataset was stained using a 36-plex antibody panel (Supplementary Table
5).

Cell segmentation was carried out using StarDist, and the mean
fluorescence intensities were quantified using in-house scripts. Hoechst
cycles were used to filter out the detached tissue areas and cells that
moved between cycles. Staining artifacts were removed by manual
cropping. The quantified data were further preprocessed: log
transformation, z-score normalization, 99.9 percentile outlier removal,
and co-factor 5 arcsinh transformation [(Hickey et al.,
2021)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?Tuc7St) were applied before
phenotype assignment. In the NACT dataset, 976,082 cells were annotated
using a cell phenotyping logic table based on panel design. For the HRP
dataset, approximately 10.5M cells were annotated using a constant logic
table.

## Methods for comparison and evaluation metrics

We compared the performance of Tribus with two similar prior
knowledge-based cell-type calling tools, ACDC [(Lee et al.,
2017)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?nMMQlc) and Astir [(Geuenich
et al., 2021)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?1m7okT). ACDC allows
prior knowledge-based cell type calling by generating landmark points
based on the knowledge table and performing semi-supervised
classification on single cells. Astir is a deep recognition neural

ased cell type calling by generating landmark points
based on the knowledge table and performing semi-supervised
classification on single cells. Astir is a deep recognition neural
network that performs fast inference of cell types based on prior
knowledge of the marker proteins.

We evaluated the overall clustering performance for all cell types by
comparing the Rand Index [(Rand,
1971)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?XrD40k), Accuracy, Precision
score (weighted), and F1 score (weighted) [(Powers,
2020)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?419Lek). All the above
metrics were calculated using the functions provided in Scikit-learn
[(Pedregosa et al., 2011)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?4aKZIs).

The Rand Index was chosen to measure the similarity between two data
clusters and was defined as $RI = (a + b)/(\frac{N}{2}$. Accuracy was
defined as $Accuracy\ = \ (TN + TP)/(TN + FN + TP + FP)$. F1-score can
reflect the harmonic mean of precision (purity) and recall (yield),

# RESULTS

## Overview of Tribus

Tribus is a hierarchical framework for cell-type assignment in
multiplexed image datasets based on prior panel knowledge (Fig 1). The
prior knowledge is provided as a logic table, where markers are
specified as "positive", "negative" or "unknown" for each cell type.

When running the analysis, Tribus first performs unsupervised clustering
using a self-organizing map on the input dataset if the number of cells
in the given dataset is larger than the user-setting threshold.
Otherwise, Tribus directly calculates the scoring function for each cell
type at the single-cell level. The labeling process of cell-type
assignment using Tribus considers ambiguous results, which means that
Tribus does not force naming. If the scores of a cluster are lower than
a certain threshold for all cell types, it is marked as 'unknown'.

ders ambiguous results, which means that
Tribus does not force naming. If the scores of a cluster are lower than
a certain threshold for all cell types, it is marked as 'unknown'.
Similarly, if the cell type scores are closer than a threshold, the
cluster is marked as \"undefined\" to avoid incorrect assignments and
facilitate the identification of new cell types.

## Tribus recovers fine-grained cell types as accurately as human experts

To test whether Tribus can recover canonical cellular populations, we
applied Tribus to the benchmark dataset DCIS, which contained 23 cell
types from a cohort of 79 patients. Tribus successfully identified all
populations highlighted in the study (Supplementary Figure 1A), and the
median marker intensities of cellular populations were similar to those
of human-labeled populations (Fig 2A). One significant difference was
the population of MACS (macrophages) which showed a lower median
intensity of CD14 in Tribus than in manual gating. This might be because
CD14 was not constrained for MACS identification in DCIS research; CD14
was not constrained to be positive for MACS cells in the logic table.

To compare how the cell type varied between manual labels and Tribus
labels, we mapped cell masks back to the original tiff images with a
nine-color overlay of cell identity-related makers. The cell masks

ype varied between manual labels and Tribus
labels, we mapped cell masks back to the original tiff images with a
nine-color overlay of cell identity-related makers. The cell masks
looked similar when we checked all major cell types (Fig 2B). We then
validated the MACS population by visualizing the original images using
six related markers. We observed that more cells were assigned to MACS
in manual labels; however, the corresponding MACS markers were absent in
some cells (Fig 2B). With Tribus, we identified fewer MACS cases, but
with canonical marker combinations.

Comparing manual labels and Tribus labels, supervised UMAP visualization
of all cell types from DCIS datasets showed that Tribus could accurately
annotate the majority of cells (Fig 2C). Using the manual labels as
ground truth, Tribus achieved precision scores between 0.7 and 0.8,
average recalls around 0.6, and F1 scores between 0.6 and 0.7 across
most of the cell types (Supplementary Figure 1B).

## Tribus can target ambiguous populations and discover phenotypically new subtypes

One challenge for prior knowledge-based cell-type calling methods is to
discover ambiguous categories that are difficult to predefine in the
logic table. For example, there are groups of unknown cells with low
intensity among all the markers. The DCIS dataset is a good example of

that are difficult to predefine in the
logic table. For example, there are groups of unknown cells with low
intensity among all the markers. The DCIS dataset is a good example of
exploring ambiguous populations because it includes "IMMUNEOTHER" and
"OTHER" cell types in manual gating. Among them, the \"OTHER\" cell type
shows low marker intensity in all markers while \"IMMUNEOTHER\" was only
highly expressed in CD44 and CD45.

We verified that Tribus can successfully target ambiguous populations by
setting the decision thresholds in the scoring function. We explored
different settings of thresholds and compared their effects on Tribus
performance (Supplementary Figure 2A-B). Based on the result, we chose
the undefined_threshold of 0.001 and other_threshold of 0.04 in the
analysis of DCIS. From the UMAP visualization, we could observe the same
"IMMUNEOTHER" and "OTHER" cell clusters with the same local structures
being kept (Fig 2C). The marker expression heatmap shows that
Tribus-targeted ambiguous cell types have the same marker expression
profile as manually gated cell types, such as "IMMUNEOTHER" and "OTHER"
(Fig 2D). We also discovered new clusters from the UMAP plots
highlighted with orange boxes (Fig 2C), including cell types of
undefined tumors, fibroblasts, and T-cells.

Novel marker expression combinations were observed using a median marker

ghlighted with orange boxes (Fig 2C), including cell types of
undefined tumors, fibroblasts, and T-cells.

Novel marker expression combinations were observed using a median marker
expression heatmap (Fig 2D). We validated our observation by mapping
cell masks back to tiff images (Supplementary Figure 3 A-C). We
identified CD36+CD31+ fibroblasts and HER2- tumor luminal subtypes.
Undefined T-cells have phenotypically higher marker expression than
typical CD4T cells. We suggest that Tribus be considered as a starting
point to uncover novel cell states via further unsupervised clustering.

## Tribus outperformed other similar methods

We benchmarked the performance of Tribus using two other approaches,
ACDC and Astir.

For AML and BMMC datasets, we used the knowledge tables provided by ACDC
research and reformatted the logic tables/yaml files accordingly for
Tribus/Astir. No knowledge tables are available for the DCIS and HubMAP
datasets; thus we generated the knowledge tables/files for all methods
based on the panel information. The ACDC analysis was performed with
parameters (n_neighbor = 10, thres = 0.5) in the example scripts. The
parameters for Astir analysis were chosen (max_epochs = 1000,
learning_rate = 2e-3, initial_epochs = 3) based on the tutorial provided
in Colab.

Benchmarking experiments showed that Tribus outperformed the other

e chosen (max_epochs = 1000,
learning_rate = 2e-3, initial_epochs = 3) based on the tutorial provided
in Colab.

Benchmarking experiments showed that Tribus outperformed the other
methods in terms of efficiency and accuracy (Fig 3A). In the AML and
BMMC datasets, Tribus reached a similar accuracy to ACDC, but with a
shorter running time of one order of magnitude (Fig 3B). Tribus
outperforms other methods across metrics on highly multiplexed imaging
datasets (Fig 3C), such as DCIS and HubMAP, and has comparable
performance in non-multiplexed AML and BMMC datasets. Tribus
successfully identified all the cell types highlighted in the four
public datasets (Supplementary Figure 4 A-D).

We then explored how the structure of the logic table affected the
accuracy. Using the AML dataset as an example, we applied Tribus
analysis with (1) a logic table with only one global level and (2) a
logic table that includes major cell types at the global level and
sub-phenotypes (for example, CD16- and CD16+ NK cells) at the second
level (Supplementary Table 1-3). We repeated the Tribus analysis five
times, calculated the average metrics, and visualized the results for
each logic table. When adjusting the logic table for the AML dataset,
using a hierarchical logic table can help improve the accuracy and
increase the F1 score by 0.1 (Fig 3D).

using a hierarchical logic table can help improve the accuracy and
increase the F1 score by 0.1 (Fig 3D).

## Tribus yields rapid and accurate cell type calling in large whole slide imaging datasets

For the HRP dataset, we used a constant one-level logic table for all 21
samples, intending to identify tumor, stromal, CD4 Tcell, CD8 Tcell,
CD11c+ myeloids, and IBA1+myeloids (Supplementary Figure 5A). We applied
Tribus to the NACT dataset guided by a four-level logic table
(Supplementary Figure 5B).

In the HRP dataset, Tribus accurately identified six cell types and the
cellular proportion showed high heterogeneity among patients (Fig 4A).
In the NACT dataset, Tribus successfully identified major cell
phenotypes as well as subtypes that we were able to characterize based
on the available marker panel (Fig 4B). The UMAP projection shows clear
cell-type populations in the HRP and NACT datasets (Fig 4C-D). We also
visualized the UMAP projection colored by sample origin on the HRP and
NACT datasets (Supplementary Figure 5C-D), and the results indicated a
low batch effect in the analysis.

We showed that Tribus can successfully annotate dense tissue areas and
create accurate phenotype labels for further analysis. The multiplexed
tCyCIF image from sample 06 of the NACT dataset showed the nuclei and

sfully annotate dense tissue areas and
create accurate phenotype labels for further analysis. The multiplexed
tCyCIF image from sample 06 of the NACT dataset showed the nuclei and
representative tumor and immune markers (Fig 4E). Tribus could
accurately identify the CD20+, CD8a+, and CD4+ cell populations within a
Tertiary Lymphoid Structure (TLS) despite the dense organization of
these sub-phenotypes (Fig 4F). Tribus also annotated the proliferating
subpopulation of tumor cells and tumor-infiltrating CD8+ cells from a
dense area (Fig 4G).

# DISCUSSION

Cell-type calling and feature extraction are critical steps in
high-dimensional image analysis. The growing complexity and increasing
number of panels in high-dimensional data necessitate the development of
reproducible and more automated cell-type calling approaches. In this
study, we developed Tribus, which is a semi-supervised cell-type calling
analysis framework for multiplexed imaging datasets. Tribus has
advantages in terms of efficiency, accuracy, user-friendliness, and
reproducibility.

Tribus was generated as an automatic cell-type caller for
high-dimensional data by incorporating biological knowledge from the
panel design into the analysis. This was done through carefully designed
scoring functions based on marker expression per grid generated by
unsupervised machine learning, to minimize bias. When the number of

is. This was done through carefully designed
scoring functions based on marker expression per grid generated by
unsupervised machine learning, to minimize bias. When the number of
cells included in the clusters was lower than the threshold set by the
user, Tribus skipped generating the clusters and calculated the scores
at the single-cell level. This flexible scoring function calculation
strategy enables the discovery of rare cell types. Tribus was integrated
with Napari, and a plugin was provided to import the cell-type
identification result in one click, which significantly increased the
simplicity of interactive quality control. Such integration enables
convenient operation for users who are not familiar with programming and
the interpretation of biological contexts. Tribus was specifically
crafted for high-plex imaging and proteomic technologies and was
seamlessly operated across various imaging platforms.

However, Tribus is not without limitations. Tribus performance is highly
related to the quality of the input dataset and prior knowledge of the
expected cell types in the user-defined initial cell-type logic table.
To assign a uniform logic table, the samples should have even staining
patterns, both within and across slides. Uneven staining patterns and
antibodies with suboptimal signal-to-noise ratios can heavily affect the
results of Tribus.

have even staining
patterns, both within and across slides. Uneven staining patterns and
antibodies with suboptimal signal-to-noise ratios can heavily affect the
results of Tribus. In sub-optimal cases, the user can create a
hierarchical logic table in such a way that major cell phenotypes with
clear marker signals are separated first on different levels. This can
result in more accurate labels if the distinct areas of the image are
affected. Tribus cannot find cell types that do not exist in the input
logic table, but it can return undefined cell types for further
exploration with unsupervised clustering methods.

Moreover, cell-type annotation from bioimages has inherent challenges
owing to imperfect cell segmentation and collateral spillover. Expanding
nuclei masks by a few pixels can enhance cytoplasmic marker visibility,
as it allows better capture of the signal when cells express these
markers. However, in dense tissue areas, this might increase spillover
which highlights the importance of the hierarchically structured logic
table. Careful design of the table can aid cell separation as only a
subset of cells are taken into account on the lower levels during the
phenotype assignment. Typically, mild spillover affects only part of a
cell, whereas a true signal yields a more uniform expression pattern and
higher mean fluorescence intensity.

phenotype assignment. Typically, mild spillover affects only part of a
cell, whereas a true signal yields a more uniform expression pattern and
higher mean fluorescence intensity. Tribus was designed to include both
positive and negative components of the expected marker expression. We
could also aid correct labeling in the case of a mild spillover using a
less strict logic table, where markers with false positive expressions
are set as neutral.

Overall, we propose Tribus as a fast, accurate, and user-friendly
cell-type identification method that can be integrated into multiplexed
image-analysis frameworks.

# Key points

- A semi-supervised cell-type identification computational tool for
 > imaging data was developed, enabling fast and accurate phenotyping
 > of single cells.

- The tool was integrated with the Napari plug-in to accomplish a
 > user-friendly assessment of the resulting quality before
 > downstream analysis.

- Compared with other similar methods, our method performed the best
 > on all benchmark datasets.

- Tribus avoids hard thresholding and allows the capture of ambiguous
 > cell populations, thus enabling novel biological discovery in
 > downstream analysis.

**Multiplexed imaging at single-cell resolution is becoming increasingly
useful to decipher the role of cellular microenvironment in cancer and
other complex diseases. To identify spatial patterns of single cells on
a tissue we must first assign accurate descriptions to each cell in a
step known as *cell-type phenotyping*. This step is challenging due to
(i) laborious annotation of ground truth, (ii) segmentation artifacts,

curate descriptions to each cell in a
step known as *cell-type phenotyping*. This step is challenging due to
(i) laborious annotation of ground truth, (ii) segmentation artifacts,
(iii) fluorescence noise and batch effects, and (iv) difficulty to
reproduce human-biased thresholding. Here we present *Tribus*, an
interactive, *knowledge-based classifier* that avoids hard-set
thresholds and manual labeling, is robust to noise, and takes less
iterations from the user than standard labeling of clustering results.
Interactive analysis is done via a Napari plugin, and each analysis
creates a detailed report to enable reproducibility. In this study we
show that *Tribus* compares to human knowledge in public benchmarking
datasets where manual cell type annotations are supported by the
pathology community. We applied *Tribus* on a dataset consisting of
cyclic immunofluorescence (CyCIF) images of six matched ovarian cancer
samples collected before and after neoadjuvant chemotherapy. Accurate
cell-type phenotyping enabled a high resolution analysis of cellular
phenotypes and their spatial patterns, as well as their temporal
dynamics during platinum-taxane chemotherapy. Tribus - an easily
embeddable open-source package - thus enables accurate phenotyping of
single cells to facilitate biological discovery from highly multiplexed
images.**

# INTRODUCTION

Multiplexed imaging techniques at single-cell resolution levels, such as
cyclic immunofluorescence (CyCIF) [(Lin et al.,
2018)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?KROv5k), Co-detection by
indexing (CODEX) [(Black et al.,
2021)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?JGGE22), and multiplexed ion
beam imaging by time of flight (MIBI-TOF) [(Keren et al.,
2019)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?QIwFvh), have developed
significant advantages in understanding tissue architecture. Such
techniques allow researchers to measure dozens of proteins at
single-cell resolution while retaining the spatial information of origin
in the tissue section.

sue architecture. Such
techniques allow researchers to measure dozens of proteins at
single-cell resolution while retaining the spatial information of origin
in the tissue section. Those experiment workflows generate datasets that
include multiple features and provide novel insights to understand the
diversity of cellular phenotypes and behaviors in tissues[(Spitzer &
Nolan, 2016)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?8HFLes).

To obtain such research, cell type annotation becomes one of the most
essential tasks and a bottleneck to gaining full potential from
multiplexed imaging technologies. The main obstacles to cell-type
phenotyping on multiplexed images are the unexpected combinations of
markers, signal noise and the reproducibility limit caused by imperfect
cell segmentation, pre-processing artifacts, or tissue processing
artifacts like autofluorescence. It is hard to apply popular scRNA-seq
cell-type callers [(Xie et al.,
2021)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?QShDIL) on multiplexed
imaging datasets because they are not designed to overcome segmentation
noise, which does not exist in scRNA-seq data. The order of magnitude
difference in the number of features is also an indispensable problem.
Several markers can dominate the clustering process in multiplexed
imaging techniques, meanwhile, thousands of genes are provided to
scRNA-seq callers to overcome this problem.

.
Several markers can dominate the clustering process in multiplexed
imaging techniques, meanwhile, thousands of genes are provided to
scRNA-seq callers to overcome this problem.

Gating [(Staats et al.,
2019)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?7mgQgW) is a classical cell
type annotation method, which requires manually identifying cell types
based on the pattern of features visualized by histograms and bivariate
plots. However, along with the increasing size of the marker panel,
manual gating becomes time-consuming and sensitive to the batch effect
[(Verschoor et al., 2015)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?beDAnS),
which is no longer feasible. Unsupervised clustering tools such as
PhenoGraph [(Levine et al.,
2015)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?fqmsl5), Leiden [(Traag et
al., 2019)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?U71hm0) and DBscan
[(Ester et al., 1996)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?L0oHCj) were
applied to perform automatic data exploration [(Liu et al.,
2019)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?hnJI58), which indeed allows
fast cell population discovery. However, manual checking is still
required on the obtained clusters to assign meaningful cell types. To
obtain deeper profiling of cell types, it is usually necessary to
perform over-clustering and then merge clusters, which is both
computationally and time-consuming. An alternative approach is to use

ling of cell types, it is usually necessary to
perform over-clustering and then merge clusters, which is both
computationally and time-consuming. An alternative approach is to use
supervised or semi-supervised machine learning methods [(Abdelaal et
al., 2019)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?49sLMo), which were
developed to perform accurate auto cell-type classification, but such
methods require pre-training on manually gated datasets according to
various experimental designs, thus require extra human work and lack
extendable.

Here, we introduce the design of a novel cell-type caller named Tribus.
Tribus performs reproducible automatic cell-type annotations across
variable high-plex imaging and proteomic datasets without the need for
manual thresholding. Tribus incorporates the widely used Self Organizing
Map (SOM) [(Kohonen, 1982)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?Yl4HID)
unsupervised neural network model with a unique scoring function for
assigning cell types according to prior biological knowledge. An
integrated Napari plugin allows for fast user interaction and curation.
We validate the accuracy of Tribus on four public multiplexed imaging
and proteomic datasets generated by mass cytometry and CODEX methods. We
compare the performance of Tribus with other two similar knowledge-based
unsupervised cell-type identification approaches, ACDC [(Lee et al.,

mass cytometry and CODEX methods. We
compare the performance of Tribus with other two similar knowledge-based
unsupervised cell-type identification approaches, ACDC [(Lee et al.,
2017)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?J4be42) and Astir [(Geuenich
et al., 2021)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?zlhfxw). We show the
utility of Tribus in the analysis of an in-house tCyCIF dataset.
Overall, Tribus represents a new framework for automated cell type
calling for multiplexed imaging and proteomics techniques.

# Methods

## The structure of Tribus

To run Tribus, a marker expression table and a logic table are required
as the input. The logic table could be defined as a data matrix
$L(c_{i},\ m_{j})$ containing values $\lbrack - 1,\ 0,\ 1\rbrack$, where
$c_{i}$ is cell type ${i_{th}}^{}$ and $m_{j}$ is marker ${j_{th}}^{}$.
If a marker should be present in a specific cell type, the value in the
logic table has to be assigned by 1. If a marker is supposed to be
absent in a specific cell type, then the value in the logic table should
be assigned -1. For neutral or unknown markers, 0 should be assigned.

Tribus consists of two main parts. First, it uses unsupervised learning
to cluster the input data, then it assigns each cluster to one cell type
based on the previously described cell type description table. The cell

rst, it uses unsupervised learning
to cluster the input data, then it assigns each cluster to one cell type
based on the previously described cell type description table. The cell
type assignment is performed hierarchically, which means Tribus first
performs the assignment of higher-level cell types, then performs the
cell type calling on each higher-level cell type, to create more precise
categories.

## Unsupervised learning in Tribus

Tribus uses Self-Organising Map (SOM) for data clustering, based on the
MiniSOM package [(Vettigli,
2013/2023)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?ghMcaA). Quantization
error (Q) was to evaluate the algorithm performance, calculated by
determining the average distance of the sample vectors to the cluster
centroids.

[![](media/image2.png){width="1.5972222222222223in"
height="0.4305555555555556in"}](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=%20Q%20%3D%20%5Cfrac%7B1%7D%7Bm%7D%5Csum_%7Bq%3D1%7D%5E%7Bm%7D%7B%5C%7C%5Cboldsymbol%7B%5Cvec%7Bx%7D%5Eq%20-%20%5Cvec%7Bw_i%7D%5Eq%7D%5C%7C%7D%5E2%20#0)

[(Pölzlbauer, n.d.)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?7mS4PU). To
determine the optimal grid size we use the approach by Vesanto [(Vesanto
& Alhoniemi, 2000)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?KEe5Lj):
$G = 5$, where $N$ is the size of the input data. Sigma (spread of the

ze we use the approach by Vesanto [(Vesanto
& Alhoniemi, 2000)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?KEe5Lj):
$G = 5$, where $N$ is the size of the input data. Sigma (spread of the
neighborhood function) and learning rate are optimized by minimizing the
quantization error. The Hyperparameter tuning was implemented based on
package hyperopt [(Bergstra et al.,
2013)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?fTIX2g), where the objective
function is to minimize quantization error, and the search space for
parameters includes sigma and learning rate from 0.001 to 5.

## Cell type assignment by scoring functions

After clustering a node matrix $N(n_{k},\ v_{j})$ was generated, where
$v_{j}$ is calculated by the median expression of marker $m_{j}$ in
$n_{k}$. A scoring function based on the idea of squared error was
designed to calculate the score $s_{i}$ of certain cell type $i$ for
each node $k$:

[![](media/image6.png){width="2.1805555555555554in"
height="0.4027777777777778in"}](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=f(n_k%2C%20s_i)%20%3D%20%5Cfrac%7B1%7D%7Bk%7D%5Csum_%7Bj%7D%5B1-%7B(v_%7Bij%7D-%5Chat%7Bv_%7Bij%7D%7D)%5E2%7D'%5D#0)

where,

![](media/image1.png){width="3.0094444444444446in"
height="0.6604265091863517in"}

Instead of using the maximum value, the 99 percentile was chosen to make
Tribus more robust to outliers.

/image1.png){width="3.0094444444444446in"
height="0.6604265091863517in"}

Instead of using the maximum value, the 99 percentile was chosen to make
Tribus more robust to outliers.

Assigning node $n_{k}$ to cell type $c_{i}$ is defined by:
$c_{i} = argmax_{s_{i}}f(n_{k},\ s_{i})$

Notice if the maximum score of a cluster is smaller than a certain
threshold set by users, the cluster will be labeled as the \"other\"
cell type. Similarly, if the difference between the maximum and second
maximum score is smaller than a certain threshold, the cluster will be
labeled as \"undefined\".

## Napari plug-in

To efficiently assess and explore the cell type labeling results, we
developed a custom plugin integrated with the Napari [(Ahlers, Jannis et
al., 2023)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?ZIFqeC) framework. It
allows the user to run Tribus over one sample at a time and display the
results simultaneously, or load previously saved data. We incorporated
the zarr format to overcome the computation limitations associated with
large datasets.

Key functionalities of the Napari plugin include:

\(i\) Cell-type mask visualization: The plugin sorts and displays the
different cell-type labels as separate layers using visually distinct
colors, allowing the user to overlay them with the imaging data for
quality control.

\(ii\) Probability score visualization: The cell masks are represented

sing visually distinct
colors, allowing the user to overlay them with the imaging data for
quality control.

\(ii\) Probability score visualization: The cell masks are represented
as a color gradient of the probability score assigned by the algorithm.
This allows the user to identify and check ambiguous cells.

\(iii\) Marker intensity visualization: The median expression levels of
the desired markers are represented through gradient shading on the
segmentation mask. It allows the user to visually assess the results and
look for potential biases.

## Benchmarking Datasets

We chose four public mass cytometry and multiplexed imaging datasets
with ground truth labels to test the performance of Tribus.

### AML

This dataset contains single-cell proteomic profiles of human bone
marrow from both Acute myeloid leukemia (AML) patients and healthy adult
donors [(Levine et al.,
2015)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?a3cXqw). AML dataset was
collected by mass cytometry with a panel of 32 markers targeting 14
manually gated cell classes. When performing benchmarking, cell type
\"NotDebrisSinglets\" was removed from the analysis (N = 104184 cells).
The cell typing logic table was based on the cell type-marker table
provided in the ACDC research.

### BMMC

The cell typing logic table was based on the cell type-marker table
provided in the ACDC research.

### BMMC

BMMC dataset is from bone marrow mononuclear cells [(Bendall et al.,
2011)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?NaqDnh) profiled by mass
cytometry. The publically available dataset contains 13 markers and 24
manually gated cell populations. Following the ACDC research,
erythroblast, megakaryocyte platelet and myelocyte were merged as an
unknown population and removed from the analysis. All \"NotGated\" cells
were also removed (N = 61725 cells). The cell typing logic table was
also based on the cell type-marker table provided in the ACDC research.

### DCIS

The ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) dataset is collected by multiplexed
ion beam imaging by time-of-flight (MIBI-TOF) with a 37-plex antibody
staining panel. DCIS consists of 69672 cells, 23 cell types from tissues
containing 79 clinically annotated surgical resections [(Risom et al.,
2022)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?dJSFis), including normal
breast tissue (N = 9, reduction mammoplasty), primary DCIS (N = 58), and
IBC (N = 12). The cell typing logic table for DCIS was also generated
based on the panel design in the published study.

### HuBMAP

HuBMAP is a published Co-detection by indexing (CODEX) imaging dataset
collected from 9 donors with a total of 54 antibodies targeting 25 cell

in the published study.

### HuBMAP

HuBMAP is a published Co-detection by indexing (CODEX) imaging dataset
collected from 9 donors with a total of 54 antibodies targeting 25 cell
types [(Hickey et al.,
2023)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?SrSjJ5). HuBMAP dataset
includes eight regions (in order of trajectory from the stomach):
duodenum, proximal jejunum, mid-jejunum and the ileum from the small
bowel, and ascending, transverse, descending and sigmoid regions of the
large intestine (colon). In this research, we use the manually labeled
donor 004 from the HuBMAP dataset (N = 110635 for colon regions, N =
137652 for small bowel regions). Cell typing logic tables were generated
according to the panel design information provided in published studies.

### In-house Dataset

To further validate Tribus\'s performance, we also applied Tribus to an
in-house high-grade serous ovarian cancer dataset generated by
tissue-based cyclic immunofluorescence (tCyCIF). The use of human tissue
samples was approved by the Mass General Brigham Institutional Review
Board. Informed consent was waived due to the use of archival samples
and anonymization of the material.Two whole-slide images of tumor
sections after neoadjuvant chemotherapy (NACT) and one treatment-naive
biopsy sample were selected based on the 36-marker tCyCIF staining. The

rial.Two whole-slide images of tumor
sections after neoadjuvant chemotherapy (NACT) and one treatment-naive
biopsy sample were selected based on the 36-marker tCyCIF staining. The
cell segmentation was carried out with StarDist and the mean
fluorescence intensities were quantified by an in-house python script.
The Hoechst cycles were used to filter out detached tissue areas and
cells that moved between the cycles. Staining artifacts were removed via
manual cropping. The quantified data was further pre-processed: log
transformation, z-score normalization, 99.9 percentile outlier removal
and finally co-factor 5 arcsinh transformation [(Hickey et al.,
2021)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?lgOGjB) were applied before
phenotype assignment. A total of 976082 cells were annotated with a cell
phenotyping logic table based on the panel design. The same logic table
was applied to one of the NACT samples and the biopsy, while CD20 was
excluded from the second NACT sample due to insufficient staining
quality.

## Methods for comparison and evaluation metrics

We compared the performance of Tribus with two other similar prior
knowledge-based unsupervised cell-type calling tools: ACDC [(Lee et al.,
2017)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?nMMQlc) and Astir [(Geuenich
et al., 2021)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?1m7okT).

ed cell-type calling tools: ACDC [(Lee et al.,
2017)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?nMMQlc) and Astir [(Geuenich
et al., 2021)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?1m7okT).

We evaluated the overall clustering performance against all cell type
classifications by comparing Rand Index [(Rand,
1971)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?XrD40k), Accuracy, Precision
score (weighted) and F1 score (weighted) [(Powers,
2020)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?419Lek). All the

above metrics were calculated by functions provided in Scikit-learn
[(Pedregosa et al., 2011)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?4aKZIs).

Rand Index was chosen to measure the similarity between two data
clusterings and was defined by $RI = (a + b)/(\frac{N}{2}$. Accuracy was
defined by $Accuracy\ = \ (TN + TP)/(TN + FN + TP + FP)$. F1-score can
reflect the harmonic mean of precision (purity) and recall (yield),

# RESULTS

## Overview of Tribus

Tribus is a hierarchy framework for cell type assignment in multiplexed
image datasets using user-defined prior knowledge (Fig 1).

The preprocessing of datasets for Tribus is performed using log and
z-scale. The datasets should then be normalized via an arcsinh
transformation with a co-factor of 5 [(Hickey et al.,
2021)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?lgOGjB) (optional) and
winsorized to \[0%, 99.9%\]. If the number of cells in the given dataset
is larger than the threshold set by the user, Tribus will first perform
unsupervised clustering using the self-organizing map on the input
dataset, with tunable sigma and learning rate. Otherwise, Tribus will
directly calculate the scoring function for each cell type on a
single-cell level.

The labeling process of cell-type assignment using Tribus considers the
ambiguous results, which means Tribus does not force naming. If the
scores of a cluster are lower than a certain threshold for all cell
types, it will be marked as 'unknown'. Similarly, if the cell type

h means Tribus does not force naming. If the
scores of a cluster are lower than a certain threshold for all cell
types, it will be marked as 'unknown'. Similarly, if the cell type
scores are closer than a threshold, the cluster will be marked as
\"undefined\" to avoid incorrect assignments and facilitate the
identification of new cell types.

## Tribus recovers fine-grained cell types as accurately as human experts

To test whether Tribus can recover canonical cellular populations, we
applied Tribus to the benchmarking dataset DCIS, which contains 23 cell
types from a cohort of 79 patients. Tribus successfully identified every
population highlighted in the study and the median marker intensity of
each cellular population showed virtually similar to human labels (Fig
2A).

To compare how cell type varied between manual labels and Tribus labels,
we mapped cell masks back to original tiff images with a nine-color
overlay of major makers and observed few differences (Fig 2B). The one
significant exception was a population of MACS (macrophages) that showed
a lower median intensity of CD14 in Tribus than with manually gating
(Fig 2A). Considering CD14 was marked positive or negative for MACS
identification in DCIS research, we didn\'t constrain CD14 to be
positive for MACS cells in the logic table. We then validate the

ing CD14 was marked positive or negative for MACS
identification in DCIS research, we didn\'t constrain CD14 to be
positive for MACS cells in the logic table. We then validate the
population of MACS by visualizing the original images with all six
marker panels related. We observed that more cells were assigned to MACS
in manual labels, but the corresponding MACS markers were absent in some
cells (Fig 2B).

Compared among manual labels and Tribus labels, Supervised UMAP
visualization of all cell types from DCIS datasets shows that Tribus can
accurately annotate the majority of cells (Fig 2C). Using the manual
labels as ground truth, Tribus achieved precision scores between 0.7 and
0.8, average recalls around 0.6, and F1 scores between 0.6 and 0.7
across all cell types (Supplementary Figure X).

## Tribus can target ambiguous populations and discover phenotypically new subtypes

One challenge for prior knowledge-based cell-type calling methods is to
discover ambiguous categories that are hard to predefined in the logic
table. For example, groups of unknown cells that have low intensity
among all markers. DCIS dataset is a good example of exploring the
ambiguous population since it includes "IMMUNEOTHER" and "OTHER" cell
types in manual gating. Among them, the \"OTHER\" cell type shows low
marker intensity in all markers while \"IMMUNEOTHER\" is only highly

includes "IMMUNEOTHER" and "OTHER" cell
types in manual gating. Among them, the \"OTHER\" cell type shows low
marker intensity in all markers while \"IMMUNEOTHER\" is only highly
expressed in CD44 and CD45.

We verified Tribus could successfully target ambiguous populations by
setting the decision thresholds (Supplementary Figure X) in the scoring
function. In the UMAP visualization, we could observe the same
"IMMUNEOTHER" and "OTHER" cell clusters with the same local structures
being kept (Fig 2C). Marker expression heatmap shows Tribus targeted
ambiguous cell types have the same marker expression profile compared
with manual gating cell types (Fig 2D).

Meanwhile, we also discover new clusters from UMAP plots highlighted
with orange boxes (Fig 2C), including cell type of undefined tumor,
fibroblast, and T-cell. Novel marker expression combinations were
observed from the median marker expression heatmap (Fig 2D). We found
CD36+CD31+ fibroblast and HER2- tumor luminal subtypes. The undefined
T-cells have phenotypically higher marker expression compared with
typical CD4T cells. We suggest that Tribus could be considered as a
starting point to uncover novel cell states via further unsupervised
clustering.

## Tribus outperformed other similar methods

We benchmarked the performance of Tribus using several orthogonal
approaches.

## Tribus outperformed other similar methods

We benchmarked the performance of Tribus using several orthogonal
approaches. We chose two prior knowledge-based unsupervised tools: ACDC
and Astir.

The benchmarking experiments show that Tribus outperforms the other
methods comparing efficiency and accuracy (Fig 3A, B). In the AML and
BMMC datasets, Tribus reached a similar accuracy with ACDC but with a
shorter running time in one order of magnitude. When it comes to
high-complexity datasets such as DCIS or HubMAP (Fig 3C), Tribus
maintains relatively high accuracy while still low time-consuming.

We also explored how the structure of the logic table affects the
accuracy using the AML dataset as an example. We apply Tribus on the AML
dataset with (1) a logic table with only one global level and (2) a
logic table that includes major immune cell types at the global level,
and further divides phenotypes (e.g. CD16- NK cells and CD16+ NK cells)
in the second level. For each logic table, we repeat Tribus analysis
five times, calculate average metrics, and visualize the result. When
adjusting the logic table on the AML dataset (supplementary material X),
using a hierarchical logic table can help improve the accuracy and
increase the F1 score by 0.1 (Fig 3D).

adjusting the logic table on the AML dataset (supplementary material X),
using a hierarchical logic table can help improve the accuracy and
increase the F1 score by 0.1 (Fig 3D).

## Tribus yields rapid and accurate cell type calling in a large whole slide imaging dataset

We applied Tribus on the in-house dataset guided by a four-level logic
table. The hierarchical logic table structure can help to improve
phenotype assignment in case of unexpected staining patterns. We
demonstrate this through the labeling of B cells: We observed that the
CD20 antibody also stained the nuclei of the cancer cells which resulted
in abundant false positive B cells when the phenotypes were assigned
based on a one-level table. Creating a hierarchical order of phenotypes
and separating cancer cells before assigning the immune sub-types
improved our results significantly.

Tribus successfully identified major cell phenotypes as well as the
subtypes which we were able to characterize based on the available
marker panel. Tumor proliferation was characterized by Ki67 and myeloid
cells were categorized into macrophage type 1 (M1) and type 2 (M2) by
CD163 expression levels. We show that Tribus can successfully annotate
dense tissue areas and create accurate phenotype labels for further
analysis.

# DISCUSSION

Cell type calling and feature extraction are critical steps in
high-dimensional image analysis. [The growing complexity and increasing
numbers of panels in high-dimensional data necessitate the development
of reproducible, more automated cell-type calling approaches.]{.mark} In
this study, we developed Tribus, a semi-supervised cell-type calling
analysis framework for multiplexed imaging datasets. Tribus shows
striking advantages in efficiency, accuracy, user-friendliness, and
reproducibility.

[Tribus was generated as an automatic cell-type caller for
high-dimensional data by incorporating biological knowledge from panel
design into the analysis. This was done through carefully designed
scoring functions based on marker expression per grid generated by
unsupervised machine learning, aiming to minimize bias.]{.mark} When the
number of cells included in clusters is lower than the threshold set by
the user, Tribus will skip generating the clusters and calculate the
scores at the single-cell level. Such a flexible scoring function
calculation strategy enables the discovery of rare cell types. Tribus
was integrated with Napari and a plugin was provided for importing the
cell type identification result in one click, which significantly

s the discovery of rare cell types. Tribus
was integrated with Napari and a plugin was provided for importing the
cell type identification result in one click, which significantly
increased the simplicity of interactive quality control. Such
integration enables convenient operations for users who are not familiar
with programming and the interpretation of biological contexts. [Tribus
was specifically crafted for high-plex imaging and proteomic
technologies, and it seamlessly operates across various imaging
platforms.]{.mark}

However, Tribus is not without limitations. Tribus performance is highly
related to the quality of the input dataset and the prior knowledge of
expected cell types in the user-defined initial cell type logic table.
To assign a uniform logic table the samples should have even staining
patterns both within and across slides. Uneven staining patterns and
antibodies with sub-optimal signal-to-noise ratio can heavily affect
Tribus's results. In sub-optimal cases, the user can try to create a
hierarchical logic table in such a way that major cell phenotypes with
clear marker signals are separated first, on different levels. This can
result in more accurate labels if distinct areas of the image are
affected. Tribus cannot find cell types that do not exist in the input
logic table, but it can return undefined cell types for further

te labels if distinct areas of the image are
affected. Tribus cannot find cell types that do not exist in the input
logic table, but it can return undefined cell types for further
exploration with unsupervised clustering methods.

Moreover, cell-type annotation from bioimages has inherent challenges
due to imperfect cell segmentation and collateral spillover. Expanding
nuclei masks by a few pixels can enhance cytoplasmic marker visibility,
as it allows to capture the signal better when cells express these
markers. However, in dense tissue areas this might increase spillover
which highlights the importance of the hierarchically structured logic
table: Carefully designing the table can aid cell separation as only a
subset of cells are taken into account on the lower levels during the
phenotype assignment. Typically, mild spillover affects only part of a
cell, while a true signal yields a more uniform expression pattern and
higher mean fluorescence intensity. Tribus is designed to include both
positive and negative components for expected marker expression. We
could aid correct labeling as well in case of a mild spillover by a less
strict logic table, where markers with false positive expression are set
as neutral.

Overall, we propose Tribus as a fast, accurate, and user-friendly
cell-type identification method that could be integrated into
multiplexed image analysis frameworks.

as neutral.

Overall, we propose Tribus as a fast, accurate, and user-friendly
cell-type identification method that could be integrated into
multiplexed image analysis frameworks.

# Key points

- A semi-supervised cell-type identification computational tool for
 > imaging data was constructed, enabling fast and accurate
 > phenotyping of single cells.

- The tool was integrated with the Napari plug-in to accomplish
 > user-friendly assessing result quality before downstream analysis.

- Compared with classic and other similar methods, our method
 > performed best on all benchmarking datasets.

- The method is capable of capturing the spatial patterns of
 > biological functions in multiple and complex cell types.

# Reviewers\' Comments to Author

Reviewer: 1

Comments to the Author

This paper proposes a cell-type caller named Tribus, which performs
automatic cell-type annotations across variable high-plex imaging and
proteomic datasets. Tribus incorporates an unsupervised neural network
model, i.e. self-organizing map (SOM), with a scoring function to assign
cell types according to prior biological knowledge. Moreover, Tribus
utilizes an integrated Napari plugin for fast user interaction and
curation. The authors demonstrate experimental results on four public
mass cytometry and multiplexed imaging datasets. However, due to the
l[ack of strong innovation and unconvincing experimental
comparisons]{.mark}, I am not convinced to accept this paper. My
detailed comments are listed as follows:

1\. The authors\' review of multiplexed imaging technologies and

imental
comparisons]{.mark}, I am not convinced to accept this paper. My
detailed comments are listed as follows:

1\. The authors\' review of multiplexed imaging technologies and
cell-type annotation is not sufficiently comprehensive, particularly as
it fails to include **several advanced works from the past two years**.

This comment is relevant to the introduction part. The advanced works in
the past 2 years include **location-based cell-type callers** such as
**CELESTA** - which we didn't mention in the introduction part. Also
STELLAR (supervised). **MAPS**.

Think about why we develop Tribus instead of using other methods: e.g.
CELESTA is unsupervised, why not directly use CELESTA in our projects?
And then improve the writing of the introduction.

**Indicate why we choose those methods to benchmark**.

2\. In the Abstract, the authors describe the proposed approach as an
interactive knowledge-based classifier. However, after reading the
entire paper, it is unclear how the algorithm interacts with humans. The
authors need to clarify the significance of emphasizing the
\"**interactive**\" aspect.

Improve the writing: "interactive" includes (1) a convenient
user-interaction interface provided by the Napari plugin, (2) Simple

Reviewer: 2

Comments to the Author

Introduction:

This manuscript introduces Tribus, a new computational tool for cell
type identification in multiplexed imaging and proteomic data.
Multiplexed imaging techniques have had the potential to improve
understanding of the impact of cellular programming on healthy and
pathologic tissue architecture and cellular functions. However, accurate
and efficient cell-type annotation remains a significant challenge and
bottleneck in analyzing these complex datasets. Tribus aims to address
this challenge by providing a semi-automated, knowledge-based approach
for cell phenotyping that avoids manual thresholding and labeling.

The development of Tribus addresses a critical need in the field of
single-cell analysis of multiplexed imaging data. As the authors note,

voids manual thresholding and labeling.

The development of Tribus addresses a critical need in the field of
single-cell analysis of multiplexed imaging data. As the authors note,
existing methods for cell type annotation often struggle with issues
like fluorescence noise, segmentation artifacts, and the need for
laborious manual annotation. By providing a more automated and
reproducible approach, Tribus has the potential to significantly
accelerate analysis workflows and improve the consistency of results
across studies.

Rigor:

The authors have demonstrated the rigor of their approach through
several key aspects:

Benchmarking against existing methods: Tribus was compared to two
similar knowledge-based cell type identification tools (ACDC and Astir)
across four public datasets. The results show that Tribus achieves
similar or better accuracy while being significantly faster.

Validation on diverse datasets: The method was tested on both public
benchmark datasets and in-house datasets, covering different imaging
modalities (MIBI-TOF, CODEX, tCyCIF) and biological contexts.

Comparison to manual annotation: For the DCIS dataset, the authors
provide a detailed comparison of Tribus results to expert manual
annotations, demonstrating high concordance.

Exploration of edge cases: The manuscript examines Tribus\' ability to

ovide a detailed comparison of Tribus results to expert manual
annotations, demonstrating high concordance.

Exploration of edge cases: The manuscript examines Tribus\' ability to
handle ambiguous cell populations and potentially discover novel
phenotypes, showing its flexibility beyond simple classification.

Usefulness:

Tribus appears to **be highly useful** for the field due to several
factors:

Speed and scalability: The benchmarking results show that Tribus is
significantly faster than comparable methods, which is crucial for
analyzing large whole-slide imaging datasets.

User-friendliness: The integration with Napari for visualization and
quality control makes Tribus accessible to researchers without extensive
programming experience.

Flexibility: The hierarchical logic table approach allows for adaptation
to different experimental designs and marker panels.

Open-source availability: By making Tribus freely available, the authors
encourage adoption and further development by the community.

Evidence of Improvement:

The manuscript provides several lines of evidence that Tribus represents
a significant improvement over existing methods:

Performance metrics: Across multiple datasets, Tribus achieves similar
or better accuracy (as measured by Rand Index, Accuracy, Precision, and
F1 score) compared to ACDC and Astir, while being consistently faster.

e datasets, Tribus achieves similar
or better accuracy (as measured by Rand Index, Accuracy, Precision, and
F1 score) compared to ACDC and Astir, while being consistently faster.

Handling of ambiguous populations: The authors demonstrate that Tribus
can effectively identify and label ambiguous or novel cell populations,
which is a challenge for many existing methods.

Scalability to large datasets: The successful application to whole-slide
imaging datasets with millions of cells showcases Tribus\' ability to
handle the scale of modern multiplexed imaging experiments.

Integration of biological knowledge: The logic table approach allows for
the incorporation of prior knowledge about marker expression patterns,
potentially leading to more biologically meaningful classifications.

Reproducibility: By avoiding hard thresholds and manual gating, Tribus
should produce more consistent results across different users and
laboratories.

While the improvements are significant, **it would strengthen the
manuscript to see more direct comparisons of the novel [biological
insights]{.underline} gained using Tribus versus other methods**,
particularly for the in-house datasets. [Additionally, exploring how

approach]{.mark}.

**Abstract**

**Multiplexed imaging at single-cell resolution has been widely adapted
to decipher the role of the tumor microenvironment in cancer and other
complex diseases. Accurate cell-type phenotyping based on marker

gle-cell resolution has been widely adapted
to decipher the role of the tumor microenvironment in cancer and other
complex diseases. Accurate cell-type phenotyping based on marker
combinations is crucial but challenging because of (i) fluorescence
noise and batch effects, (ii) segmentation artifacts, (iii) laborious
annotation of the ground truth, and (iv) difficulty in reproducing
human-biased thresholding. Here, we present Tribus, an interactive
knowledge-based classifier designed for multiplexed image and proteomic
datasets that avoids hard-set thresholds and manual labeling. We
validated the efficiency and accuracy of Tribus by benchmarking with two
similar methods on four public datasets with manual labels. Tribus can
recover fine-grained cell types as accurately as human experts, while
allowing the discovery of phenotypically new subtypes. We selected two
large in-house whole-slide image datasets to show Tribus yields rapid
and accurate cell type calling in practical applications. Tribus is an
open-source package and comes with a user-friendly Napari plug-in
enabling integration with image analysis workflows for fast
visualization and interpretation for biological discovery from highly
multiplexed images.**
